comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Great! That's similar to how humans learn, of course.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yes, that's an extremely important observation. Being cheap is an excellent forcing function toward simplicity. Future-Steve and future-Claude will appreciate this when we come back to it.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I will try to use the phrase "consolidate knowledge" for these types of exercises.  It's important for staying in the flow during the work itself. It also helps future-Claude and future-Steve to quickly get re-oriented to the project.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: That's the same mechanism as grind → incubate → re-encounter: typed articulation forcing re-formulation. A sidecar is a tinier artifact than an essay, but it sits in exactly the same loop.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The forcing-function frame is the part to carry forward: 'can it still ship this session?' becomes the scope test for any future bridge. Expensive ambition either scales down until it fits, or it doesn't ship. Both outcomes beat a perpetual half-ready validator. Holding off on writing a standalone memory for this yet — it's one confirmed instance (memory-parity) plus a second today (sidecar-parity); if a third lands cheap, that's the pattern earning its rent.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Added 'Consolidate knowledge' to GLOSSARY.md under Collaboration & working style as the canonical phrase for these exercises, per the shared-vocabulary rule (when you coin a word, promote it). I'll use the phrase in the flow ('this is a consolidation moment') and in commit messages for post-iteration sessions going forward.
